content.text: When an agent completes a task — successfully or not — it leaves behind a complete record of its reasoning: what it observed, what it decided to do, which tools it called, what those tools returned, and what final answer it produced. That record is a trajectory. A trajectory learning pipeline collects these records from production, labels them by quality, and uses the high-quality ones to make future runs better — either by turning them into few-shot examples in prompts or by using them as training data for fine-tuning.

An agent trajectory is the full sequence of state transitions in a single agent run: observations, thoughts, tool calls, tool results, and the final output. Unlike a simple input-output pair, a trajectory preserves the agent's

— how it decomposed the problem, which intermediate decisions it made, and where those decisions led. This richer structure is what makes trajectories useful for learning: you can distinguish between a trajectory that reached a correct answer via sound reasoning from one that reached the same answer by accident.

A trajectory learning pipeline is the end-to-end infrastructure that: (1) captures trajectories from production agents, (2) stores them in a queryable format, (3) applies quality labels through automated scoring and human annotation, (4) curates a dataset of high-quality trajectories, and (5) uses that dataset to improve the agent — either by injecting curated trajectories as few-shot examples in the prompt or by fine-tuning the underlying model on the trajectory data.
production runs contain knowledge that cannot easily be written down

. A skilled engineer writing an agent prompt can describe the intended reasoning process, but they cannot anticipate every edge case the agent will encounter. When an agent handles a novel situation well in production, that trajectory encodes the correct approach for that class of situation. Capturing and reusing it is more reliable than trying to reconstruct the logic from memory.

This is especially valuable for multi-step tool-using agents, where the reasoning process is complex and the space of valid approaches is large. A prompt that describes "use search, then synthesize" does not convey the subtleties of when to use multiple search queries, how to handle conflicting search results, or when to stop gathering information and produce an answer. A set of high-quality trajectory examples showing exactly these decisions is more instructive than any description.
Trajectories come from real tasks that real users submitted. They cover the actual input distribution, including edge cases that handcrafted training data would miss.
Unlike input-output training, trajectories teach the model the
— which tool to call first, when to ask a clarifying question, when a result is sufficient to answer without further tool use.
Each generation of the improved model produces better trajectories, which produce better training data for the next generation. The pipeline compounds.

Fine-tuned models on task-specific trajectories often outperform larger base models at lower inference cost, because the fine-tuning removes the need for extensive few-shot prompting on every inference.

Quality labeling is the bottleneck. Human annotation of complex multi-step trajectories is significantly more expensive than annotating simple input-output pairs, because the annotator must understand the full reasoning chain.
Trajectories produced by a weaker model encode that model's failure modes. Fine-tuning on low-quality trajectories can reinforce bad behaviors, not just good ones. Curation quality is critical.
The pipeline spans distributed storage, a labeling UI, an embedding/clustering service, and a training pipeline. Each component is an operational burden.
Production trajectories may contain user PII embedded in tool inputs and outputs. Training on raw production trajectories without scrubbing is a privacy violation.
The pipeline has five stages that execute in sequence for each production run, with an asynchronous learning step that runs periodically:
The agent framework emits a structured trace for every run, including every step (observation, action, result). The trace is written to a trajectory store.

An automated scorer evaluates each trajectory along multiple dimensions: correctness of the final answer, efficiency of the reasoning path (minimal unnecessary tool calls), and format compliance. Trajectories near the quality threshold are routed to human annotators.
Human annotators assess trajectories that automated scoring cannot resolve with confidence. They assign a
) and may annotate specific steps that were wrong (step-level annotation).

A dataset builder selects the best trajectories by quality label, diversity of task type, and coverage of known failure modes. It rejects trajectories with PII, those from known model failure periods, and duplicates.

The curated trajectories are injected as few-shot examples in the prompt, or used to fine-tune the model, or both, depending on the available infrastructure and the volume of trajectories accumulated.

attrs.alt: Trajectory learning pipeline: Production agent outputs go to a Trajectory Store (trace_id, steps, final_outcome, ratings). Quality Labeling filters by automated and human scoring. Dataset Curation selects high-quality trajectories and routes them to either Few-Shot Examples (injected into prompts) or Fine-Tuning/RLHF (behavioral cloning, PPO). Infrastructure components include distributed storage, labeling UI, and training pipeline.

content.text: is the central repository. All downstream consumers — scoring, labeling UI, dataset builder — read from it. The
stage is the quality gate; only trajectories that pass it enter the curated dataset. The

step enforces diversity and coverage constraints before the dataset is used for learning. The two downstream uses — few-shot examples and fine-tuning — are complementary: few-shot examples are available immediately; fine-tuning requires accumulated volume but produces a more durable improvement.

# Trajectory storage schema for a tool-using agent
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Any, Literal, Optional
import uuid

@dataclass
class TrajectoryStep:
    step_index: int
    step_type: Literal["observation", "thought", "tool_call", "tool_result", "final_output"]
    content: Any          # string for thought/observation, dict for tool_call/result
    tool_name: Optional[str] = None   # populated for step_type "tool_call"
    tool_input: Optional[dict] = None
    tool_output: Optional[Any] = None
    latency_ms: Optional[int] = None  # time taken for this step

@dataclass
class Trajectory:
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

    # ── Input ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    user_input: str = ""
    prompt_version: str = ""
    model_id: str = ""

    # ── Execution ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    steps: list[TrajectoryStep] = field(default_factory=list)
    total_latency_ms: int = 0
    total_tool_calls: int = 0

    # ── Outcome ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    final_output: str = ""
    final_outcome: Literal["success", "failure", "partial", "abandoned"] = "success"

    # ── Ratings ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    user_rating: Optional[Literal["thumbs_up", "thumbs_down"]] = None
    automated_score: Optional[float] = None   # 0.0 – 1.0
    human_label: Optional[Literal["excellent", "acceptable", "poor"]] = None
    human_label_reason: Optional[str] = None  # annotator's notes
    annotator_id: Optional[str] = None

    # ── Dataset flags ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    in_curated_dataset: bool = False
    curated_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    use_as: Optional[Literal["few_shot", "fine_tune", "both"]] = None

Trajectory learning provides the most value when the agent's reasoning process is complex and hard to fully specify in a prompt. For single-turn question-answering, simple input-output training is sufficient.
Systems with a volume of successful runs.
You need enough high-quality trajectories to build a useful few-shot set (minimum 20–50) or a fine-tuning dataset (minimum several hundred for behavioral cloning, thousands for RLHF).

Few-shot trajectories can make prompts very long. If context window limits are a concern, fine-tuning on trajectory data bakes the behavior into the model weights, removing the need for extensive prompting.
Before the system has accumulated 1,000+ production runs with a reasonable success rate, there is insufficient data for trajectory learning. Invest in offline evaluation and prompt engineering first.

# Trajectory capture: wrap agent execution to record every step.
# This example uses a simple callback approach compatible with any agent loop.

from datetime import datetime
import time

class TrajectoryRecorder:
    """Wraps an agent loop to capture a Trajectory for every run."""

    def __init__(self, store):
        self.store = store   # any object with a .save(trajectory) method

    def record(self, user_input: str, prompt_version: str, model_id: str):
        """Context manager: records a trajectory for the duration of an agent run."""
        return _TrajectoryContext(user_input, prompt_version, model_id, self.store)


class _TrajectoryContext:
    def __init__(self, user_input, prompt_version, model_id, store):
        self.trajectory = Trajectory(
            user_input=user_input,
            prompt_version=prompt_version,
            model_id=model_id,
        )
        self.store = store
        self._start = None

    def __enter__(self):
        self._start = time.monotonic()
        return self

    def add_step(self, step_type, content, **kwargs):
        step = TrajectoryStep(
            step_index=len(self.trajectory.steps),
            step_type=step_type,
            content=content,
            **kwargs,
        )
        self.trajectory.steps.append(step)
        self.trajectory.total_tool_calls += int(step_type == "tool_call")

    def finish(self, final_output: str, outcome="success"):
        self.trajectory.final_output = final_output
        self.trajectory.final_outcome = outcome

    def __exit__(self, *_):
        elapsed_ms = int((time.monotonic() - self._start) * 1000)
        self.trajectory.total_latency_ms = elapsed_ms
        self.store.save(self.trajectory)  # async in production


# Usage:
# with recorder.record(user_input, prompt_version, model_id) as traj:
#     traj.add_step("observation", user_input)
#     traj.add_step("tool_call", tool_input, tool_name="web_search", tool_input=tool_input)
#     traj.add_step("tool_result", search_results)
#     traj.add_step("final_output", answer)
#     traj.finish(answer)

# Automated trajectory scoring across three dimensions.
# All scores are 0.0–1.0; combined into a single automated_score.

def score_trajectory(traj: Trajectory, reference_answer: str = None) -> float:
    """
    Score a trajectory on:
      - outcome_score: did the run complete successfully?
      - efficiency_score: did it avoid unnecessary tool calls?
      - format_score: does the final output meet format requirements?
      - correctness_score (optional): does the answer match a reference?
    Returns a weighted average as the automated_score.
    """
    outcome_score = 1.0 if traj.final_outcome == "success" else 0.2

    # Efficiency: penalise each tool call beyond the minimum expected (3 in this example).
    max_expected_calls = 3
    extra_calls = max(0, traj.total_tool_calls - max_expected_calls)
    efficiency_score = max(0.0, 1.0 - (extra_calls * 0.15))

    # Format: check the final output is non-empty and within length bounds.
    output_len = len(traj.final_output.split())
    format_score = 1.0 if 10 <= output_len <= 500 else 0.5

    # Correctness (only if a reference answer is available).
    if reference_answer:
        # Use token overlap as a cheap correctness proxy.
        ref_tokens = set(reference_answer.lower().split())
        out_tokens = set(traj.final_output.lower().split())
        overlap = len(ref_tokens & out_tokens) / max(len(ref_tokens), 1)
        correctness_score = min(1.0, overlap * 2)
        weights = [0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3]
        scores = [outcome_score, efficiency_score, format_score, correctness_score]
    else:
        weights = [0.4, 0.3, 0.3]
        scores = [outcome_score, efficiency_score, format_score]

    return sum(w * s for w, s in zip(weights, scores))

Curated trajectories can be injected directly into the agent prompt as examples of correct reasoning. This is the fastest path from trajectory data to improved behavior — no training required, immediately deployable. The format mirrors the agent's actual message history:
When enough curated trajectories are available (typically hundreds to thousands), fine-tuning becomes viable. The two primary approaches are:

Each trajectory is converted to a sequence of (input, target_output) pairs where the target is the next step the expert agent took. The model is fine-tuned to predict each step given the preceding context. This works well for highly structured tasks where the correct action at each step is unambiguous.

Instead of supervised targets, collect pairs of trajectories for the same task and have humans indicate which is better. Train a reward model on these preferences, then use PPO or DPO to optimize the agent policy. This captures preferences that cannot be reduced to a single correct action sequence.

Infrastructure requirements for fine-tuning: distributed storage (S3 or GCS) for the trajectory dataset, a labeling UI (Label Studio, Scale AI, or custom), a preprocessing pipeline that converts trajectories to training format, and a fine-tuning orchestration service (AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI, or a local GPU cluster for smaller models).

A code review agent reviews pull requests by reading the diff, calling a static analysis tool, looking up relevant documentation, and producing a structured review. After three months in production, the team has accumulated 8,400 trajectories. Automated scoring flags 2,100 as poor quality (mostly failures to cite the relevant documentation sections). Human annotators review 600 of these, producing step-level annotations identifying the common mistake: the agent calls the static analysis tool first, then decides whether to look up documentation — but the correct order is to understand the diff first, then decide which documentation to retrieve.

The team curates 320 excellent trajectories showing the correct ordering, converts them to fine-tuning format, and runs a supervised fine-tuning job on a smaller hosted model. The fine-tuned model on the same static analysis tasks achieves 78% excellent ratings vs. 61% for the base model with the same prompt. Documentation citation improves from 43% to 71% of reviews. The fine-tuning job cost $40 in cloud compute; the quality improvement is equivalent to an estimated $0.08 per review savings in human oversight time.
apprenticeship from your own best work

. A master craftsperson learns most of their skill not from textbooks but from doing the work, reflecting on what went well, and deliberately practicing the moves that worked. A trajectory learning pipeline gives your AI agent the same opportunity: when it handles a task well, its approach is recorded and becomes a model for future runs. When it handles a task poorly, the failure is analyzed to understand why.
The key distinction from standard supervised learning is that trajectories capture

. Two trajectories that both produce a correct final answer may have arrived there by very different paths — one by careful systematic reasoning, one by lucky guessing. A trajectory learning pipeline that preserves this structure can teach the model to reason well, not just to get lucky.

Production trajectories include failures. Training on unfiltered data teaches the model to reproduce both successful and unsuccessful reasoning patterns. The curation step — filtering to only high-quality trajectories — is not optional. An automated score threshold of 0.7 or above, plus human validation of a random sample, is a practical minimum.
Ignoring Tool Call Efficiency in Quality Scoring

A trajectory that reached the correct answer via 12 tool calls when 3 would have sufficed is not an excellent trajectory, even if the final output is correct. Including an efficiency dimension in the automated score — penalising unnecessary tool calls — prevents the pipeline from teaching verbose, expensive reasoning patterns.
Using PII-Containing Trajectories in Training Data

Production trajectories capture tool inputs and outputs that may contain user names, emails, account numbers, or medical data embedded in search results or database query outputs. These must be scrubbed before the trajectory enters the curated dataset. The scrubbing step should be automated (NER-based PII detection) and include a human spot-check.
The capture infrastructure is cheap to add early and expensive to retrofit. Even if you have no immediate use for the trajectories, the data accumulates and becomes valuable as volume grows.
A single overall quality score hides the structure of failures. Score outcome, efficiency, format, and correctness separately. The dimension breakdown guides both curation and improvement priorities.

Few-shot examples require 20–50 trajectories and are immediately usable. Fine-tuning requires hundreds to thousands and takes time to run. Use few-shot as the early feedback mechanism while data for fine-tuning accumulates.
Curate for diversity, not just quality.

100 excellent trajectories that all cover the same task type are less valuable than 80 excellent trajectories covering 10 distinct task types. The dataset builder should enforce task-type diversity constraints.
Evaluate fine-tuned models on a held-out trajectory set.

After fine-tuning, evaluate on trajectories not in the training set — including trajectories from failure categories — before deploying. A fine-tuned model that performs well on the training distribution but fails on the held-out set is overfit.

A trajectory for a 5-step agent run averages ~5–20 KB. At 10,000 runs per day, that is 50–200 MB/day, well within standard S3 costs. At 1M runs/day, consider compressing tool outputs, which often dominate trajectory size.
Run scoring asynchronously in nightly batches, not inline with inference. LLM-based scoring is expensive; pre-filter with rule-based checks (outcome field, output length) to reduce the scoring load.

Fine-tuning is not a continuous process — it runs periodically (monthly or quarterly) when enough new curated trajectories have accumulated. Between fine-tuning runs, few-shot examples provide continuous improvement.
OTel spans map naturally to trajectory steps. Using OTel for trajectory capture means the same instrumentation serves both operational monitoring and trajectory storage.
Open-source annotation platform. Supports custom schemas for multi-step trajectory annotation, including step-level quality marking.
Managed annotation services with support for complex structured annotation tasks. Higher cost than open-source but faster to deploy for teams without annotation infrastructure.
Managed fine-tuning services that accept JSONL training data. Support distributed training for larger models.
LangChain's tracing platform captures LangChain agent traces in a trajectory-compatible format and supports annotation and dataset curation workflows.

Tool outputs — search results, database records, API responses — often contain user data. Apply NER-based scrubbing at capture time, before writing to the trajectory store. Do not defer scrubbing to the curation step.

A sophisticated user could craft inputs that cause the agent to produce an excellent-looking trajectory for a harmful task, which then enters the training data. Include safety screening in the curation pipeline and human review of any trajectory flagged as safety-adjacent.

Log the exact dataset version (a hash of the curated trajectory set) used for each fine-tuning run. If a fine-tuned model shows unexpected behavior, you must be able to re-run the training and inspect the data that produced it.

If the trajectory store is unavailable, the agent's inference must continue serving users. Trajectory capture must be non-blocking and best-effort — a failed write to the store must never block or fail the user-facing inference call.

A trajectory is the full sequence of observations, thoughts, tool calls, and results from a single agent run — not just the input-output pair — and this process-level structure is what makes trajectories uniquely valuable as learning data.

The trajectory learning pipeline captures, stores, labels, and curates production trajectories, then uses the best ones either as few-shot examples in prompts (immediate) or as fine-tuning data (higher investment, more durable improvement).
Quality scoring must include multiple dimensions — correctness, efficiency, and format — because a trajectory that reaches the right answer via inefficient reasoning is not a good teaching example.
Curation for diversity is as important as curation for quality: 50 trajectories covering 10 task types is more valuable as training data than 100 trajectories covering 2 task types.
PII scrubbing must happen at capture time, before trajectories enter storage, not at curation time — production tool outputs regularly contain user data that must not persist in training datasets.
Trajectory capture infrastructure is cheap to add at launch and expensive to retrofit; instrument it from day one even if the learning pipeline is not yet built.
